title: Bayesian inference and role of astrocytes in amyloid-beta dynamics with modelling of Alzheimer's disease using clinical data
abstract: Alzheimer's disease (AD) is a prominent, worldwide, age-related neurodegenerative disease that currently has no systemic treatment. Strong evidence suggests that permeable amyloid-beta peptide (Abeta) oligomers, astrogliosis and reactive astrocytosis cause neuronal damage in AD. A large amount of Abeta is secreted by astrocytes, which contributes to the total Abeta deposition in the brain. This suggests that astrocytes may also play a role in AD, leading to increased attention to their dynamics and associated mechanisms. Therefore, in the present study, we developed and evaluated novel stochastic models for Abeta growth using ADNI data to predict the effect of astrocytes on AD progression in a clinical trial. In the AD case, accurate prediction is required for a successful clinical treatment plan. Given that AD studies are observational in nature and involve routine patient visits, stochastic models provide a suitable framework for modelling AD. Using the approximate Bayesian computation (ABC) approach, the AD etiology may be modelled as a multi-state disease process. As a result, we use this approach to examine the weak and strong influence of astrocytes at multiple disease progression stages using ADNI data from the baseline to 2-year visits for AD patients whose ages ranged from 50 to 90 years. Based on ADNI data, we discovered that the strong astrocyte effect (i.e., a higher concentration of astrocytes as compared to Abeta) could help to lower or clear the growth of Abeta, which is a key to slowing down AD progression.

introduction: The human brain is a system with billions of neurons that is incredibly complex and is managed by cognitive processes. Neurodegenerative diseases (NDDs), such as AlzheimerвЂ™s disease (AD), are defined by a progressive pathological transformation of the brainвЂ™s biochemical processes and anatomy, which eventually leads to permanent impairment of cognitive functioning . Amyloid plaques, neurofibrillary tangles, neuropil threads, dystrophic neurites, cerebral amyloid angiopathy, and glial responses are examples of вЂњpositiveвЂќ lesions that accumulate in the brains of AD patients . Plaques are extracellular deposits of the amyloid-ОІрќ›Ѕ\beta peptide (AвЂ‹ОІрќђґрќ›ЅA\beta) that are created when the amyloid precursor protein is sequentially cleaved by the enzymes ОІрќ›Ѕ\beta-secretase and Оірќ›ѕ\gamma-secretase . Years before AD symptoms appear, plaque begins to accumulate in the neocortex, then moves on to the hippocampus, diencephalon, striatum, brainstem, and lastly the cerebellum . The formation and deposition of the AвЂ‹ОІрќђґрќ›ЅA\beta peptide are largely thought to be responsible for the development of AD . The temporal evolution and spatio-temporal distributions of AвЂ‹ОІрќђґрќ›ЅA\beta in AD have been broadly characterized ex vivo through neuropathologic studies utilizing AвЂ‹ОІрќђґрќ›ЅA\beta immunostaining . In terms of the temporal evolution of AвЂ‹ОІрќђґрќ›ЅA\beta in AD, studies suggest that the accumulation of AвЂ‹ОІрќђґрќ›ЅA\beta plaques in the brain may occur years or even decades before the onset of clinical symptoms . Moreover, AвЂ‹ОІрќђґрќ›ЅA\beta has a regular spatiotemporal distribution that is initially limited to a few specific brain regions, for instance, in the cerebral cortex of patients with AD, before spreading further in the disease state . Importantly, the main hypothesis that is often used is that AвЂ‹ОІрќђґрќ›ЅA\beta growth starts in a limited number of affected brain areas and then spreads to other brain regions during the course of the disease via mechanisms like prion-like self-propagation or transsynaptic spread . The in vivo regional AвЂ‹ОІрќђґрќ›ЅA\beta concentration can be assessed in humans using the ADNI database (https://adni.loni.usc.edu/). There have been large-cohort cross-sectional studies on ADNI for AвЂ‹ОІрќђґрќ›ЅA\beta growth, but the research in this context has mostly concentrated on identifying the different clinical stages of the disease . The amount of knowledge on the evolution of AвЂ‹ОІрќђґрќ›ЅA\beta plaques over time is still quite limited, despite recent approvals by the FDA of two new drugs in 2021 (aducanumab) and 2023 (lecanemab). In particular, longitudinal analysis has been limited to small time frames of no more than two years or the average deposition throughout the whole brain . Therefore, as such, the spatiotemporal distributions of AвЂ‹ОІрќђґрќ›ЅA\beta have not been completely defined yet despite the huge number of in vivo data that have been gathered and analyzed. It is known that abnormal AвЂ‹ОІрќђґрќ›ЅA\beta aggregation, astrogliosis, and reactive astrocytosis are major pathogenic hallmarks of AD . Interestingly,
methodology: It has been found that astrocytes, which are abundantly present in the brain, can play a vital role in slowing down the progression of AD. Studies have suggested that a strong astrocyte effect can help clear the growth of AвЂ‹ОІрќђґрќ›ЅA\beta while weak astrocyte effects can contribute to an increase in its production . The present Section highlights the deterministic and statistical modelling approaches incorporating the strong and weak astrocyte effects on AвЂ‹ОІрќђґрќ›ЅA\beta growth via a model applied within the Bayesian setting. This is achieved by defining the stochastic growth model of AвЂ‹ОІрќђґрќ›ЅA\beta to account for individual correlated time series using ADNI data. Additionally, we simulate the growth trajectories of the stochastic model of AвЂ‹ОІрќђґрќ›ЅA\beta using likelihood estimation. This is followed by the parameter estimation and uncertainty quantification presented in Sub-section 2.3. 2.1 Deterministic growth model of AвЂ‹ОІрќђґрќ›ЅA\beta incorporating strong and weak astrocyte effects Fig. 1 gives an overview of our methods. As can be seen, our end goal is to focus on the stochastic growth model of AвЂ‹ОІрќђґрќ›ЅA\beta because of the inherent stochasticity of monomers growth processes in the regime of small AвЂ‹ОІрќђґрќ›ЅA\beta peptides numbers . However, the structure of the functional forms of the kinetic equations describing the deposition of AвЂ‹ОІрќђґрќ›ЅA\beta proteins in the brain can be understood fairly well within a deterministic framework. Therefore, this step will link us to the historical and most widely implemented mathematical models of AD in neuroscience. In the present study, we consider the following three deterministic models (given in Eqs. (1), (3-4)) that describe the kinetic growth of AвЂ‹ОІрќђґрќ›ЅA\beta. The first model is the baseline model of AвЂ‹ОІрќђґрќ›ЅA\beta growth, in which the growth rate dвЂ‹AвЂ‹ОІвЂ‹(t)dвЂ‹tрќ‘‘рќђґрќ›Ѕрќ‘Ўрќ‘‘рќ‘Ў\frac{dA\beta(t)}{dt} is proportional to the number of AвЂ‹ОІрќђґрќ›ЅA\beta, and a single growth rate constant, rрќ‘џr, resulting in the standard exponential growth model: dвЂ‹AвЂ‹ОІвЂ‹(t)dвЂ‹t=rвЂ‹AвЂ‹ОІвЂ‹(t),рќ‘‘рќђґрќ›Ѕрќ‘Ўрќ‘‘рќ‘Ўрќ‘џрќђґрќ›Ѕрќ‘Ў\frac{dA\beta(t)}{dt}=rA\beta(t), (1) where AвЂ‹ОІвЂ‹(t)=AвЂ‹ОІвЂ‹(0)вЂ‹erвЂ‹tрќђґрќ›Ѕрќ‘Ўрќђґрќ›Ѕ0superscriptрќ‘’рќ‘џрќ‘ЎA\beta(t)=A\beta(0)e^{rt} and AвЂ‹ОІвЂ‹(0)рќђґрќ›Ѕ0A\beta(0) is the initial value of AвЂ‹ОІрќђґрќ›ЅA\beta at time tрќ‘Ўt. This model represents the evolution of AвЂ‹ОІрќђґрќ›ЅA\beta monomers that display individual autonomous proliferation provided by the growth rate constant rрќ‘џr across time and AвЂ‹ОІрќђґрќ›ЅA\beta as shown in Fig. 2 (a) for initial values of AвЂ‹ОІрќђґрќ›ЅA\beta equals [200;400;600]вЂ‹mвЂ‹L200400600рќ‘љрќђї[200;400;600]mL. We choose these values for AвЂ‹ОІвЂ‹(0)рќђґрќ›Ѕ0A\beta(0) so that they would be consistent with the ADNI data for AD patients who had baseline (bl) to 222-year visits (i.e., AвЂ‹ОІвЂ‹(0)=200вЂ‹mвЂ‹Lрќђґрќ›Ѕ0200рќ‘љрќђїA\beta(0)=200mL at baseline, AвЂ‹ОІ12в€’monthв€’visitsвЂ‹(57)=400вЂ‹mвЂ‹Lрќђґsubscriptрќ›Ѕ12monthvisits57400рќ‘љрќђїA\beta_{\rm 12-month-visits}(57)=400mL at 121212 months, and AвЂ‹ОІ24в€’monthв€’visitsвЂ‹(57)=600вЂ‹mвЂ‹Lрќђґsubscriptрќ›Ѕ24monthvisits57600рќ‘љрќђїA\beta_{\rm 24-month-visits}(57)=600mL at 242424 months are the initial values for the mean of AвЂ‹ОІрќђґрќ›ЅA\beta at three visits). results: The temporal probabilistic distribution of AвЂ‹ОІрќђґрќ›ЅA\beta provides valuable information for studying the progression of AD and developing effective treatments through the ADNI database. In this section, we will present the results obtained from the developed stochastic models presented in Section 2.2. In AD, the accumulation of AвЂ‹ОІрќђґрќ›ЅA\beta plaques is known to be a key pathological feature of the disease. The spatial distribution of these plaques throughout the brain is known to be heterogeneous, with some regions being more affected than others. Additionally, the temporal progression of the accumulation of AвЂ‹ОІрќђґрќ›ЅA\beta plaques can also vary, with some patients showing a more rapid progression of the disease than others. The ADNI database contains data on patients with varying degrees of AD, and as a result, the AвЂ‹ОІрќђґрќ›ЅA\beta accumulation patterns may differ across different patients. Moreover, the temporal aspect of AвЂ‹ОІрќђґрќ›ЅA\beta accumulation in AD refers to the progression of the disease over time, as the accumulation of AвЂ‹ОІрќђґрќ›ЅA\beta and the formation of plaques gradually spread throughout the brain, and this progression can vary among individuals. Our models produce the temporal evolution of AвЂ‹ОІрќђґрќ›ЅA\beta using simulated data and the ADNI data. Moreover, random noise with a standard deviation of 505050 is added to the simulated trajectories to account for variability in the data as shown in Fig. 3 (a). conclusion: AlzheimerвЂ™s disease (AD) is probably the most common cause of dementia. Although the mechanism behind the onset and progression of AD is not fully known, mounting evidence points to a complex etiology that may involve a variety of genetic, environmental, and age-related variables. Increasing evidence suggests that astrocytes play an important and active role in many neurodegenerative disorders. Given the growing interest in normal astrocyte biology and the numerous research studies on variations in astrocyte function in the context of neurodegenerative diseases, it may come as a surprise that there are still no well-developed therapies that utilize astrocytes as primary targets. One of two pathological hallmarks of AD is the deposition of amyloid-ОІрќ›Ѕ\beta (AвЂ‹ОІрќђґрќ›ЅA\beta) in the brain. The temporal evolution of AвЂ‹ОІрќђґрќ›ЅA\beta has been widely explored ex vivo. As a starting point, a logistic growth model can be used to simulate the in vivo temporal distribution of AвЂ‹ОІрќђґрќ›ЅA\beta in AD. Our ultimate objective was to concentrate on the stochastic growth model of AвЂ‹ОІрќђґрќ›ЅA\beta due to the intrinsic stochasticity of AвЂ‹ОІрќђґрќ›ЅA\beta concentrations. However, the structure of the functional forms of the kinetic equations describing the deposition of AвЂ‹ОІрќђґрќ›ЅA\beta proteins in the brain can be understood rather well within a deterministic framework. Therefore, firstly, we developed three deterministic models for AвЂ‹ОІрќђґрќ›ЅA\beta growth, viz., simple growth (Eq. 1), weak (Eq. 3), and strong (Eq. 4) astrocyte effect models. Then, we applied a stochastic modelling approach to analyze the evolution of AвЂ‹ОІрќђґрќ›ЅA\beta in vivo using ADNI data from AD patients to investigate the effect of astrocytes on AвЂ‹ОІрќђґрќ›ЅA\beta spread in AD. We detailed the stochastic approach using the quantitative template for the progression of the AD project data set generated from the baseline to 222-year samples visits from ADNI1 and ADNIGO/2. Specifically, we used the AвЂ‹ОІрќђґрќ›ЅA\beta data for 170617061706 individuals with 688068806880 visits and clinical follow-up visits to fall within the 242424-month window and analyzed the growth of AвЂ‹ОІрќђґрќ›ЅA\beta with respect to age. Importantly, for the present study, we chose the data for the patients who have already developed AD. The approximate Bayesian computation (ABC) technique was used to determine whether the aforementioned stochastic models (i.e., sвЂ‹MвЂ‹1в€’sвЂ‹MвЂ‹3рќ‘ рќ‘Ђ1рќ‘ рќ‘Ђ3sM1-sM3 presented in Table 1 along with mean and variance presented in Table 2) were fitted well or not. The stochastic trajectories of AвЂ‹ОІрќђґрќ›ЅA\beta data were sampled from the bвЂ‹lрќ‘Џрќ‘™bl to 222-years, corresponding to the age utilized. In order to simulate the ADNI data as closely as possible, the growth of AвЂ‹ОІрќђґрќ›ЅA\beta, as well as the mean and variance, were calculated at each time point. In summary, the effectiveness of astrocytes in clearing AвЂ‹ОІрќђґрќ›ЅA\beta in AD was studied using stochastic growth models fitted to ADNI data.
